name: Optical Illusion Gallery -- What Does Your Brain Add?
description: Explore optical illusions and discuss what your brain is actively adding or interpreting. Why does the illusion work? What assumptions is your brain making about what you see?
materials: printed optical illusions or digital display
learning_objectives: Understand that perception is active, not passive
Recognize that the brain interprets sensory information
Discuss how expectations shape what we perceive

name: Attention Blindness Challenge -- Can You See the Gorilla?
description: Watch a video where you're asked to count passes in a basketball game. Most people miss a person in a gorilla suit walking through the scene. This classic experiment shows that attention is selective—we miss what we don't expect.
materials: video of inattentional blindness experiment (freely available online)
learning_objectives: Understand that attention is selective and limited
Recognize that we miss information we don't expect
Discuss real-world implications (driving distractions, eyewitness testimony)
variations: Discuss implications for safety and learning

name: Study Strategy Experiment -- Testing What Actually Works
description: Design and test different study methods on the same material: cramming, spaced repetition, active recall (practice testing), and interleaving. Compare which works best. What does research say? Why?
materials: study material (vocabulary list, concept definitions, or facts)
learning_objectives: Understand that study strategies have different effectiveness
Apply research-backed methods (spaced repetition, active recall, interleaving)
Develop metacognition about personal learning
variations: Test with different material types (facts vs. concepts)

name: Memory Palace Construction -- Ancient Memory Technique
description: Build your own memory palace (method of loci). Mentally walk through a familiar place (your home, school, a park). Place images representing information you want to remember in specific locations. Walk through your palace mentally to retrieve information.
materials: list of information to remember (vocabulary, concepts, historical dates)
learning_objectives: Understand encoding and retrieval strategies
Apply spatial memory to organize information
Recognize how visualization aids memory
variations: Teach the method to someone else

name: Emotion Thermometer Journal -- Daily Emotional Check-In
description: Daily practice: Check in on your emotional state. Rate intensity (1-10). Name the emotion(s). Identify the trigger. Notice where you feel it in your body. Over time, recognize patterns in your emotional life.
learning_objectives: Build emotional awareness and vocabulary
Understand emotions as information with causes
Notice patterns and triggers in own emotional life
variations: Draw emotions instead of naming them

name: Motivation Mapping -- Intrinsic vs. Extrinsic
description: List activities you do regularly (hobbies, schoolwork, chores, sports). For each, identify: Are you motivated by intrinsic rewards (enjoyment, personal growth, purpose) or extrinsic rewards (grades, money, approval)? Which feel more energizing? Why?
materials: motivation map template (can be simple or visual)
learning_objectives: Understand intrinsic vs. extrinsic motivation
Recognize what activities feel most meaningful to you
Think strategically about building sustainable motivation
variations: Redesign an extrinsic task to be more intrinsically rewarding
Explore what happens to motivation when extrinsic rewards are removed

name: Interview Across Generations -- Understanding Development
description: Interview people at different life stages (child, teen, adult, older adult). Ask: What's important to you? What challenges are you facing? What have you learned? How are you different than when you were younger? Reflect on what you learn about development.
learning_objectives: Understand developmental stages through real voices
Recognize how priorities and challenges shift across lifespan
Build empathy and appreciation for different life stages
variations: Analysis of common themes across interviews

name: Perspective-Taking Story Rewrite -- How Different Ages Understand Events
description: Read a scenario about a family or social conflict. Rewrite it from the perspective of a 5-year-old, a 13-year-old, an adult, and someone aging. How does understanding shift? What matters to each age group? Why?
materials: age-specific perspective notes (what matters at each stage)
learning_objectives: Understand cognitive and emotional development stages
Recognize how perspective shifts with age and experience
Build empathy for how others understand the world
variations: Discuss real family conflicts through developmental lens

name: Conformity Observation Challenge -- When Does a Group Influence You?
description: Design a subtle test of conformity. Example: In a group, everyone looks up at the sky for no reason. Do strangers join? Or: Sit the wrong way on a bus seat. Do people comment? Observe when people conform to group behavior, and why. Reflect on your own conformity without judgment.
materials: notes on conformity and social influence
learning_objectives: Understand social influence and conformity
Recognize conformity in real situations
Think critically about when conformity is adaptive vs. problematic
variations: Compare conformity across settings (school, online, family)
Discuss how to resist unhealthy conformity
Examine media messages and conformity pressure

name: Empathy Circle Discussion -- Deep Listening & Perspective-Taking
description: Gather in a circle. One person shares their perspective on a social situation or conflict. Others listen deeply without interrupting or judging. Reflect back what they heard. Then others share their perspectives. The goal is understanding, not agreement.
materials: conflict or social situation to discuss
empathy circle guidelines (speaking order, respectful listening)
learning_objectives: Practice active, empathetic listening
Understand multiple perspectives on a situation
Develop communication skills for difficult conversations
variations: Written perspectives for those uncomfortable speaking
Follow-up on how understanding each other changed relationships

name: Emotion Regulation Toolkit -- Strategies for Managing Feelings
description: Learn and practice emotion regulation strategies: deep breathing, physical movement, cognitive reframing, talking to someone, creative expression (drawing, music), mindfulness. Test different strategies and identify which work best for you in different situations.
materials: practice guides (breathing exercises, movement ideas, reframing prompts)
learning_objectives: Understand different emotion regulation strategies
Practice strategies in safe settings
Build a personal toolkit for managing difficult emotions
variations: Create a visual reminder (poster, phone wallpaper)
Refine toolkit based on what works for you

name: Stereotype Interrupt -- Recognizing and Challenging Biases
description: Notice stereotypes in media, conversations, and your own thinking. Where do stereotypes come from? What's the impact? Practice interrupting stereotypes with individuality and complexity. Discuss how biases form and how to counter them.
materials: examples of stereotypes (in media, conversation, behavior)
prompts for thinking about origins and impacts
learning_objectives: Recognize stereotypes in media and everyday life
Understand how stereotypes affect people and relationships
Practice interrupting biases with evidence and complexity
variations: Design campaigns to counter specific stereotypes
